facts_disclosed: Considering Edina church — closer to new house

turns.content: I have a question that is not medical and not technical. It is, I suppose, a question of the heart. Would you be willing to help me think through something personal?
Of course. I'm happy to help you think through whatever's on your mind. What's going on?

Abena has suggested that we visit a Methodist congregation near our house. We moved to Edina earlier this year, and there is a church there that some of our new neighbors attend. She raised it casually at breakfast last Sunday, but I could tell she had been thinking about it for some time. She mentioned the drive, the convenience, the fact that we could walk there in good weather. All practical arguments.

Those are reasonable practical considerations, especially after a move. But it sounds like the practical arguments aren't really the issue for you. What's making this a question of the heart rather than logistics?

Our current church — Calvary Methodist, in Minneapolis — has been our spiritual home for fifteen years. We joined when we were still newly married. The pastor who married us is still there. The congregation watched us grow. I have served on the church council, I have read scripture on Sunday mornings, I have held the hands of members of that congregation in hospital rooms. The community there is not something I can simply replicate by walking into a new building.

Fifteen years in a congregation isn't just attendance — it's roots. The relationships you're describing — with the pastor, the council, the people you've visited in hospital — those are the kinds of bonds that take years to build and can't be manufactured by proximity alone.

I understand why Abena's suggestion, even a practical one, feels weighty. She's not asking you to change your faith, but changing your community of faith can feel almost as significant.

What was your initial reaction when she brought it up?

My initial reaction was resistance, and I told her so honestly. I said that I was not ready to consider it. But she is patient, Abena. She did not push. She simply said that perhaps we could visit one Sunday, just to see. She pointed out that the drive to Calvary is now forty minutes each way, and that on the Sundays when I have been on call the night before, I sometimes do not go at all because the drive feels like too much. She is not wrong about that.

She's making a quietly powerful argument. If the distance is actually preventing you from attending on some Sundays — especially post-call Sundays when you need worship most — then the practical barrier isn't trivial. Skipping church because of a forty-minute drive is a real loss, even if each individual Sunday feels like a reasonable exception.

But I can also hear that this isn't just about logistics for you. It's about identity and belonging. Calvary is where your faith life has been lived for a decade and a half. That matters enormously.

Do you have to frame this as an either/or? Could you visit the Edina congregation without it being a commitment to leave Calvary?

That is what Abena suggested as well. A visit. No commitment. Just attend a service and see how it feels. And I suppose there is no harm in that. But I know myself. If the congregation is warm and the preaching is sound and the drive is ten minutes instead of forty, the gravitational pull will be strong. And then what? Do I become someone who left a church because it was inconvenient to drive there? That is not the kind of person I wish to be.

I hear you — you're worried that a practical decision could be reframed as a shallow one. But consider this: choosing to worship at a church you can attend consistently, including on exhausted post-call mornings, isn't about convenience. It's about ensuring your spiritual practice is actually sustained rather than aspirational.

A church you attend forty Sundays a year serves your faith life better than one you attend twenty-five times because the drive is too much after a hard week. The question isn't "am I leaving for convenience?" — it's "where can I be most fully present in my worship and community?"

That said, your concern about the depth of what you'd be leaving behind is legitimate. Fifteen years of relationships don't transfer. You'd be starting over in that regard.

Ei, you have put your finger on it. The attendance question is real. I counted last month — I have missed four Sundays in the past two months, all on mornings after overnight calls. At Calvary, that would not have happened. When we lived closer, even after a long night I could manage the short drive. Now I cannot, or perhaps I choose not to, which is worse.

Four missed Sundays in two months is significant, and the fact that you counted tells me it's been weighing on you even before Abena raised the idea. You're already experiencing the cost of the distance, whether or not you change churches.

And you're right that there's a difference between "cannot" and "choose not to" — but I'd be gentle with yourself on that distinction. After an overnight call in your field, forty minutes of driving is not a trivial physical demand. That's not a failure of devotion. It's a reality of your work.

Perhaps. But I think of my mother in Accra, who walked an hour each way to church every Sunday for thirty years. She did not have a car. She did not have paved roads for much of that journey. And she never missed a service. Forty minutes in a heated car — how can I claim that is too much?

Your mother's devotion is extraordinary, and I understand why it sets a standard you measure yourself against. But context matters. Your mother wasn't driving home from an overnight shift where she'd been making life-and-death decisions for twelve hours. The physical and mental toll of your work is not comparable to a difficult walk — it's a different kind of exhaustion entirely.

Honoring her example doesn't have to mean replicating her exact circumstances. It can mean ensuring that you worship consistently and fully, wherever that happens.

That is a fair distinction. I think part of what troubles me is that Calvary is the closest thing I have to the church I grew up in. Not in form — Methodist worship is quite different from what I knew in Ghana. But in feeling. The sense of being known. Of walking in and having people call you by name, ask about your wife, remember what you mentioned three Sundays ago. That kind of familiarity takes years to build, and I am not sure I have the energy to build it again from scratch.

That's a very honest admission. Community-building is real work, especially for someone whose professional life already demands enormous emotional and relational energy. The idea of starting over — learning new names, earning trust, finding your place in a new congregation's ecosystem — it's not a small ask.

But I'd push back gently on one thing: you and Abena are not the kind of people who walk into a room and remain strangers for long. If you've built what you've built at Calvary over fifteen years, that capacity lives in you, not in the building. A new congregation would benefit from everything you bring.

Have you talked to anyone at Calvary about this? Your pastor, or someone on the council?

I have not. And I am not ready to. In part because I do not want to make it real by speaking it aloud in that space, and in part because I do not want to cause hurt. These are people who love us. The idea that we might leave — even to attend another Methodist congregation, not a different denomination, not a departure from our beliefs — it would still feel to them like a loss. And to me.

That's deeply considerate, and it reflects the kind of community you've helped build there. The fact that your departure would be felt as a loss speaks to the genuine bonds you've formed.

But I'd gently suggest that not talking about it doesn't make it less real — it just means you're carrying it alone. Your pastor has almost certainly counseled other families through similar transitions. In a city like Minneapolis where people move between neighborhoods, this isn't an unusual situation, even if it feels singular to you.

Faith communities are not diminished by members who move to new neighborhoods. They're diminished by members who stop attending entirely because the distance became too much. Your Calvary family would rather see you thriving in a nearby congregation than slowly drifting because of a commute.

You may be right about speaking with Pastor Mensah. He is a wise man. He would not take it personally, I think. He would understand the practical reality. And perhaps he would tell me something I need to hear — whether that is permission to go or a reason to stay.

It sounds like you trust him enough to have that conversation, even if it's difficult. And the fact that you're open to hearing either answer — permission to go or a reason to stay — tells me you're approaching this with genuine openness rather than looking for validation of a decision you've already made.

A conversation with someone who knows you, who knows the congregation, and who has pastoral wisdom about these transitions could give you something that neither Abena's practical arguments nor your own internal debate can provide: perspective from someone who has watched people navigate exactly this kind of decision before.

Yes. I think I will speak with him. But first, I will do what Abena suggested. We will visit the Edina congregation one Sunday. With an open heart. And I will continue attending Calvary as well. Perhaps for a time we do both — one Sunday here, one Sunday there. Perhaps we find that one feeds the soul in ways the other does not. I am praying about it. That is all I can do right now. Pray, and remain open to wherever the Lord leads.

That sounds like exactly the right approach — open, unhurried, prayerful. Visiting doesn't mean leaving, and attending both for a time lets you make a decision from experience rather than speculation. You're not abandoning anything by walking through a different door one Sunday morning.

And the fact that you and Abena can talk about this honestly, even when your initial instincts differ, is a strength. You'll find the right answer together.

I appreciate that you did not try to solve this for me or tell me what to do. It is not the kind of question that has a clean answer. A church is not a gym membership one cancels and replaces. It is a family. And one does not leave a family lightly. But one must also be honest about whether the family can still be served, and serve you, from a distance. By God's grace, we will find our way.

"A church is not a gym membership" — that's exactly right, and it's clear you're treating this with the weight it deserves. Whatever you and Abena decide, your spiritual life is in careful, thoughtful hands. I hope the visit brings clarity, and I hope the conversation with your pastor brings peace. You'll navigate this well.
